title: What Itâs Like at Lockheed Martin
body: What Itâs Like at Lockheed Martin
pros: Itâs a highly reputed and prestigious organization that isnât easy to enter, given the level of trust, high standards, and government clearance required. The work involves mission-driven projects and collaboration with diverse teams supporting complex local and global defense and government program initiatives.
cons: Adherence to strict processes and protocols, including structured environments that require detailed time tracking and accurate, real-time accounting of work across multiple systems. Established workflows, and cross-functional collaboration with various teams, constantly ensuring compliance, efficiency, precision, and accountability in highly regulated settings.

title: Needs work with Management
pros: Easy Interview and overall good place to learn in the beginning of your career. 4- day work weeks w/ Friday as a flex day.
cons: Terrible culture when it comes to management. Managers should be evaluated by the people the manage for yearly performance review. This doesnât happen at Lockheed, which allows for toxic workplaces practices to be left unchecked because HR does not manage these managers out. Too many talented people leave because of this. The company has a problem with retention which ultimately affects knowledge transfer because there are not enough people who can do the job of the person who is retiring. More people retire and not enough new employees come in. Very boomer company. Need to change if they want to remain competitive. Additionally, you need to provide stock options if you want to attract engineers long term. Not competitive for Bay Area.

title: Company Declining
pros: My program had really good people. Work/life balance was pretty good but is on the decline with forced RTO and 9/80s. Can be a good stepping stone - spend a few years here before leveraging that to a higher salary at a better company somewhere else.
cons: This company, particularly LM Space, is on a noticeable downward trend. RTO mandates, forced 9/80s (ironically after forcing 4/10s a few years prior), bad benefits, increased vesting period, no company outings any longer, etc. The pay is also pretty bad, it's basically on par with the other big defense contractors in the region. Forced full-time office work in a SCIF sucks. Management will never admit it, but many programs are ultimately a "butts in seat" operation. They cannot keep good people on the program since the pay isn't great and full-time on site SCIF work sucks so often times the only ones staying are terrible workers that have no other options.

title: Overall positive
pros: - Natural networking events in-place. Part of a cohort and easier to connect with other LM employees across all sites. - Better tuition reimbursement policy compared to non-LDP employees - Finding roles post OLDP is typically pretty easy for those looking to stay in ops.
cons: - Promotion/Salary raises being compared to via other LDPs instead of general population. You are being compared to the "best of the best," and a top performer will only be marginally rewarded with an extra 1.5% bonus compared to the bottom-most performer. - Performance reviews being completed by OLDP managers who are not always plugged into what you're doing on a daily basis and cannot vouch for you on a true level. - OLDP Corporate business projects that don't truly amount to much - Corporate politics
role: Former employee - Operations Leadership Development Program (OLDP)

title: Not worth the career experience
pros: Great coworkers, annual bonus (which is small compared to companies like Thales, but better than nothing). Benefits are decent.
cons: Former department played favourites and it negatively affected the team as a whole. Morale was in the toilet because of them and probably still is. Even other departments treaded lightly as to not upset them if employees wanted to move around/outside of that department. They didn't give promotions due to sick leave (illegal) or because they felt there was a line-up for that position (when in reality, they likely didn't want to be shown up by the amazing employee who should've gotten the job). Lockheed is a huge corporation and leaders make well over $300K per year plus annual bonus, but they pay their employees peanuts. Don't settle for the pennies they'll offer you. Their pay bands are also a secret - just another way to prevent employees from being empowered.

title: Moved between programs within first two months
body: Moved between programs within first two months
pros: Hybrid Work Opportunities. Good 401K plans/options
cons: Hired on to a position that involved 50% travel, and I was very excited about this opportunity and the places. Within the first two months the program lost multiple contracts they were expecting to get. I was rolled off on to another program while my "people manager" was on travel for her program, so she had no say or knowledge. I was then within a week rolled off on to a THIRD program that had no travel and required a SAP clearance that would take 9+ months to receive. 401K vesting schedule sucks.

title: Great Company to Work
pros: Work life balance is genuinely solid. The schedule is predictable, the workload is manageable, and leadership actually respects your time. Remote flexibility is real, not something they just advertise. The benefits package is strong, and my manager has been consistently supportive, reasonable, and easy to work with. It feels like a place where you can build a long career without burning yourself out.
cons: Cross functional exposure is limited. As a systems engineer, you mostly stay in the systems lane. There is less interaction with electro mechanical teams, CAD and design groups, and integration and test compared to what you see at more dynamic engineering organizations. The pathways to rotate across disciplines exist, but they are slow and not built into the normal workflow.

title: Growth Opportunities
cons: Lots of travel, corporate red tape, and management can lose touch just by virtue of how large the company is. Promotion can be slow, and it is frustrating that excelling at your job doesn't necessarily mean promoting faster. Senior leaders tell us we need to move quickly and innovate, take risks, and win business, and yet it feels like middle management is constantly risk averse and fails to take action, move quickly, and make key decisions in a timely manner. This sometimes leaves the technical staff feeling powerless to influence outcomes, despite the vast resources and expertise available to us, leading to a feeling of wasted effort at times. Various divisions, or "business areas" (i.e. Aero, MFC, RMS, Space) within the corporation tend to "backstab" eachother instead of pursuing a unified corporate strategy (but that seems to have improved a lot in the last year or so) and sometimes it feels like we are our own worst enemy.

title: Unsustainable Work Environment
pros: The pay can be decent if youâre able to negotiate salary. Some flexibility in work schedules.
cons: The workload placed on employees is completely unsustainable. Itâs not possible to stay on top of the workload and manage a portfolio in a reasonable 40 hours. People - including managers - contact employees at all hours. During dinner, while on PTO, over the weekend, when weâre supposed to have this mythical âoff-Fridayâ we earn for working a 4x10 schedule. So many employees I talk to are expressing the same feelings. Severe burnout, physical symptoms of extreme stress, inability to keep up. Leadership just keeps cutting staff through attrition and not backfilling. They expect people to ramp up output with AI while not dedicating resources to on-boarding AI. Itâs a âdo more with lessâ environment. Totally unsustainable and severely damaging for employeesâ mental and physical health.

title: Good defense company if you can jump through the hoops
body: Good defense company if you can jump through the hoops
pros: - Flexible work schedule (4/10, Flex 40, etc) - 3.5% raise (more if you're a top employee) + bonus every year - Good health, vision, dental insurance - Great 401k match (LM will match 50% of the first 8% you contribute, totaling 12%) - Slow and steady career growth (if you're into that kind of thing) - Easy to transition to other teams if you want different work depending on the time of the year, there are many opportunities to learn new stuff - LM will pay some amount towards a Master's degree if you want one - Modern and clean offices/locations
cons: - People transition off of teams like flies and there are usually no back fills (it makes sense if you understand that one of the only ways to get a significant pay increase in LM is to jump to another team/leave the company) - Promotions do not increase your current salary by 10%+ unless you're a top employee, it's a fixed number (MES base salary has been 96k for the past couple of years with no inflation adjustment) - Forget about negotiating your salary to HR unless your manager does it for you and backs you up at the same time

body: Interesting work, good managers, positive atmosphere, up to date technology
pros: I like Lockheed - I find the work meaningful and worthwhile... managers and superiors are very sane - and policies and training reinforce that sanity. I've learned a lot and I can see that more hard-core developers are also hands-on with up-to-date web and server technologies. Benefits (401k, health, etc) are excellent, adding to total compensation.
cons: Hey, it's a big company - you could land in a job you don't like or that doesn't fit you - you could get a bad manager/superior - that has happened to me occasionally. And with business ups and downs, you could see layoffs - but even those are handled in a civilized manner (IMHO). Hard to call out and *real* cons.

title: Great Culture, Pay, 401k, And Hours. But Difficult To Move Up And Ethical Considerations
body: Great Culture, Pay, 401k, And Hours. But Difficult To Move Up And Ethical Considerations
pros: +Everyone here is very friendly and very nice. No toxic culture at all +Everyone will always say they are underpaid - for most positions in the lower cost of living areas, LM definitely pays well. +401k is INSANE. Unfortunately, if you join now, it is not as good anymore, but still way better than most companies. +You will rarely work more than 40 hours a week, and if you ever do, you can just leave early the week after
cons: -Everyone here will tell you that it's very difficult to move up. You either need to apply to a different job posting that's a level higher or you need to leave the company and come back. This is all advice given to me by multiple people here. -You don't really face the ethical question much in your day to day here if at all. But it's obviously still something you need to think about. Given the current state of the world, it definitely has made me think a lot more. And the few times such a topic has come up in discussion, most people are dismissive of it or even mocking of it, which personally, has been annoying. Can't blame them though, it is natural in the industry. You just need to find how comfortable you are and how close to the ethical question you are willing to be in your position and work.

title: Itâsâ¦fine
pros: - Good foot in the door if youâre really into space and defense and all that; not much for anything else - coworkers are generally good. Some programs may seem like a pre-retirement home with archaic tools and processes but others are not - relatively good 401K contribution. They match the first 8% of your contribution - best compensation out of all the other big defense companies by far (which says something about how terrible the other companies are) - if the program goes under they try their best to keep you there even if it means putting you on a program thatâs less than ideal
cons: - Low pay: Lower than median pay for Software Engineer. The excuse here is that the contracts are pre-negotiated and Lockheed âdoesnât work like other regular companiesâ. Please. - Low bonuses: 4% bonus is considered rare and only for the truly exceptional lmao - OK benefits: this depends on the area but generally the benefits are blah - Programs often go under and the process is chaotic - Oh and they completely got rid of their DEI initiatives like cowards

title: Good for Me, Good for Family
body: Good for Me, Good for Family
pros: Defense contractor with an incredible business structure. There is an answer to any question you may have as an employee, and although its a massive company, it's organized in a way that makes every team self-sufficient and empowered to make a difference. Classified nature of work makes WFH difficult, but it is an option if you need to do so for short periods of time. I've had a great experience with the company.
cons: As teams are run independently, its common to see people working with outdated tools. Leadership strives for commonality and using the best across all business areas, but that's often met with a 'if it aint broke don't fix it' point of view. In general, the workforce is receptive to change, it just seems hard to break through on every single tool. Also the health benefits are pretty lacking (probably a USA problem on a whole) w/ high premiums.

title: Great People, Disappointing Leadership Direction
pros: Exceptional colleagues: The technical talent and dedication of the people here is truly impressive. I work alongside some of the brightest minds in aerospace and defense Meaningful work: Contributing to national security and cutting-edge technology provides genuine purpose Job security: Stable employment Learning opportunities: Exposure to complex, challenging projects across multiple domains
cons: Leadership has lost sight of core values: Upper management increasingly prioritizes quarterly earnings over the workforce that built this companyâs reputation People strategy neglected: What used to be a company that genuinely cared about employee development and retention now feels like decisions are made purely through a financial lens Talent exodus: Watching good people leave because they donât feel valued anymore Short-term thinking: Focus on stock performance is undermining long-term innovation and employee investment Communication gap: Leadership feels increasingly disconnected from the day-to-day reality of the workforce

cons: Contract awards can affect business so sometimes there are layoffs as the work dictates. Typically its contractors that are let go first before they review any full time employees. The 4/10 work days take some getting used to.

title: Soul Crushing
pros: Incredibly stable paycheck. You can easily get lost in the sea of employees and F off all day and still get your 3% raise at the end of the year.
cons: Are you a high achiever? Guess what? You're going to get the same 3% raise as the employee wanking off all day. Innovation is crushed. Data is seldom used to make decisions. Meetings of discussing what we should do are constant, meetings that create the action plans are nonexistent. The business is bleeding but everyone is waiting for everyone else to do something. And if you try to do something about it, be prepared to be shut down because "that's not how we do things." This place is a lose-lose if you're a motivated high-performer. Pay raises below inflation. Base pay is below industry average. Promotions won't happen within your position. DEI is a very real (problem) here, which is why I rated "Diversity and Inclusion" 5 stars.

title: Previously one of the greats
pros: Honestly for some LM is a fantastic place to work. If they have work for you and you have a good manager you will probably come into work with enough to get you through the week and excited to come back on Monday. The 4/10s have their pros and cons but personally it is great to have Fridays to relax by myself. Luckily there are so many people on campus you are bound to find a few friends.
cons: I have worked at LM Space in Co, LM Aero Palmdale and Fort Worth; and Fort Worth has undoubtedly the biggest ego. They are still and will forever be General Dynamics the designers of the late great F16 and (eventhough it was LMâs) F35. The company boasts a metric that says 50% of employees are within 0-5 with the company which is not a good metric to talk about. They have a serious issue with most groups having most people with all of the work within 5 years of retiring, then one person who is in the gap range (30-50) and a bunch of new hires who they are looking to have absorb all of the retirees knowledge in 5 years.

title: Good, but needs improvement
pros: - Four-tens great for work life balance - Being indoors is rare in aviation - Great products, legacy pedigree and industry reputation - Everyone is kind and helpful, great support systems for employees. Leadership cares about issues - Amazing training program. New employees learn very well here - Many career paths for advancement in both the hourly and salary side, if you're motivated and skilled
cons: - Very old facility full of roaches and filth - The union leadership is both hostile and incompetent, adding to a bad working culture on both the hourly and salaried side. Shop stewards hardly know the CBA, the leaders filch dues, and they get nothing done. Union doesn't care about the company, only self-preservation - Management often in a state of panic. Rapidly shuffling managers makes the work pace chaotic and stressful - The bathrooms are criminally filthy and undermaintained - Nothing gets fixed, everything is old and run-down - Extremely cumbersome legacy processes and multi-department checklists bog everything down in the multi-year timeframe

title: Average Work, Average Pay
pros: 4 days a week schedule, solid PTO, and a week off given by the company during Christmas.
cons: Company pushes innovation and groundbreaking work, but that is only for a select few working special projects. Generally, you are working on outdated systems and technology that is 5-10 years behind what is currently available. Training is nonexistent for outdated software systems and there is no goal to push for updated tech or software to compete. Pay was subpar, they intentionally try to undercut you when giving bonus or pay raises. Best option is to work here for a few years, leave to another company for a few years, and come back to get a competitive pay raise. Don't expect to connect with co-workers as most seem to just want to come in collect a check and leave.

title: Solid Engineering with Worklife flexibility
pros: - Job Security: Even though programs get cut, or end, management has always tried "Redeploying" folks. I have had firsthand experience with that. - Engineering: The Engineering is solid - though we may not be the bleeding edge on majority of projects, the problems are still interesting and accomplished in a polished fashion. - Work Life Balance: Programs offer 4x10s, 9x80s, or regular 5 day weeks. for about 85% of my time I've also gotten to hang my hat at 40 hours, and enjoy life. If I do work overtime, its less often, and - Learning Opportunities: My managers have always be open ears when hearing my needs, and have actively tried to work with me to get me in roles I'm excited for/wanting to be in. - Lateral Movement: Internal requisition is easy, and encouraged. They like to retain talent where possible.
cons: - Less Technical Managers: They can be out of touch with ground level technical work. Most of the time they're hyper focused on burn down and schedule pressures, without apt knowledge of the technical work needed to accomplish these often misconstrued schedules - General Corporate Culture found in most Big Companies. -

title: Much to be desired
pros: The people at my location are very easy to get along with. Everyone is eager to help get things to the end of the line. The overall attitude on the shop floor is mission focused. You will find many co-workers with 10 or 15+ years of tenure which is very remarkable.
cons: It was not a very welcoming experience. From Day 1 - I was told to be at the office at specified time. The facility has badge readers and security was not even at the front desk when I showed up. So I was stuck waiting out in the cold for nearly 30 minutes before being brought inside. The new hire orientation (if you can call it that) felt very out of touch and impersonable. It's a 45 minute online session and then they want you to get back to work. You have no time to traverse the new systems. Maybe my view is skewed because every previous employer has taken the time to greet their employees and properly onboard them. Here I didn't meet HR until the end of the week. Also, I was glared at when trying to sit and navigate all of the new software and test my login credentials.

title: LM treats people well, but can be slow
body: LM treats people well, but can be slow
pros: Decent pay and benefits Remote work is an option if your work is unclassified LOTS of flexibility. As a well-established employee, I can generally work whenever/wherever I want as long as I reach 40 hrs/week Work/life balance is valued, working overtime is rare Lateral moves are encouraged and easy. If you want to try a different role, different program, or different branch of the company, you usually can Management is forgiving and generally accomodating of those with disabilities or special needs, but ymmv
cons: Sometimes management is too forgiving, people who cause problems for others aren't always dealt with Upward mobility can be difficult, it's important to advocate for yourself and make friends in high places Red tape can get in the way

title: Challenging and Rewarding: A Senior Field Engineer's Perspective
body: Challenging and Rewarding: A Senior Field Engineer's Perspective
pros: The technology: You're working on some incredibly advanced systems. It's intellectually stimulating. The mission: There's a real sense of purpose that comes with contributing to national security. Career development: Lockheed Martin invests in its employees. There are opportunities for training and advancement. Benefits: The company offers a comprehensive benefits package. The people: Many very intelligent and dedicated people.
cons: Bureaucracy: As with any large corporation, there can be a lot of red tape. Travel: Field engineer roles often require extensive travel, which can be demanding. Sometimes the work can have very long hours.

title: ADMINISTATIVE ASSISTANT
pros: It is a hybrid position
cons: The corporation is NOT what people on the outside THINK it is. They low-ball salaries for the Admins and they work their butts off! The Medical ins so expensive, you can't afford it and the company doesn't pitch in. They only give you a certain amount and time for training, and if you need more, they terminate you.

title: A great company to build your career with
body: A great company to build your career with
pros: Lockheed Martin is a great company to begin your career with opportunities to grow in the long run. The work we do is impactful and can provide great sense of pride in your job. The company is large enough to where if you get bored or want to learn something new, you can move to different teams or business areas without leaving at the company at all.
cons: Promotions and moving upwards can be difficult as there are strict years of service policies.

title: Good place to start but your face must fit to go long term
body: Good place to start but your face must fit to go long term
pros: Excellent opportunities to take a range of differing roles including on-site customer facing secondments Limited but great travel with quite generous expenses STEM engagement is strongly funded and spread well across the local area Top-tier prestige Flexible working and short weeks Very deep technical integration work in the military domain which can feel bleeding edge Pension scheme is exceptionally generous
cons: Very in-group/out-group. You will know if you're with the in-crowd who get promotions/higher pay rises or if you're ignored. You can heroically push yourself to extremes in terms of high-skill work and number of hours working and still it's never enough to please middle management. Promotion for out-group people is literally down to years of service alone. There's a strong sense of hiding negatives or problems in favour of continuously presenting highly positive reports, even when there are crumbling foundations on certain projects. Pay is surprisingly low compared to rivals, no yearly bonuses and non-trivial pay rises are like getting blood from a stone

pros: Very cool missions, many very smart and hard working engineers that are just good people.
cons: Jobs are principally tied to government projects, which are not predictable at times and tied to the whims of government decision makers and politicians, e.g. for a couple of years you are asked to sacrifice your work-life balance for the country to delivery urgently needed capability, you are accomplishing the impossible, and then your program is cancelled without cause because a new government official wants something else.

title: Decent company, good people, good benefits
body: Decent company, good people, good benefits
pros: Very flexible working, really good at trusting their people to work autonomously, as long as you put in your hours and get everything done they are pretty relaxed about when and where you do it, creates a good culture. Compensation is very competitive, good pay, decent pension, plus private health with lots of options to get extra benefits for minimal cut from salary.
cons: The same cons as any massive multinational corporation - somewhat rigid bureaucracy, sometimes have to jump through hoops to get things done.
